ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for zhajiangmian; closest parent 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03311069) used as the best defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for zhajiangmian; A0FJP (Noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of zhajiangmian is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of boiled wheat noodles topped with a generous portion of fried soybean paste sauce with diced pork and fresh vegetable garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles with meat sauce (炸酱面) — Chinese Food Composition Table, Standard Edition, Vol. 1 & USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for wheat noodles with pork and soybean paste
nutrient_source.organization: China National Center for Food Safety Risk Assessment (CFSA) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for zhajiangmian as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked wheat noodles (NDB 20110), ground pork stir-fried with soybean paste, and published Chinese Food Composition Table data for 炸酱面 (approximately 155 kcal/100 g). The composite estimate reflects a typical Beijing-style preparation with ~50% noodle, ~25% sauce-meat mixture, and ~25% vegetable garnish by weight. Values are consistent with peer-reviewed dietary survey data for this dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and noodle-based dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for zhajiangmian exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 47 is estimated from the closest analogous food: wheat noodles (Chinese-style, boiled), which have a reported GI of approximately 47 (low) per Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. The soybean paste sauce and protein content of the dish are expected to further moderate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as (47 × 19.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 400 g serving) / 100 = 36.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fermented soybean paste and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Zhajiangmian contains approximately 480 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1920 mg per standard 400 g serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sauce preparations, and monitor cumulative sodium intake from other meals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles, partially offset by protein and fat in the sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 47 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 36 per standard serving, zhajiangmian poses a moderate glycemic challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control to half a standard bowl (approximately 200 g) and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help attenuate postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from ground pork and soybean paste
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (approximately 1920 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (approximately 7.6 g per serving) in zhajiangmian is relevant for cardiovascular risk management; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency, choose leaner pork preparations, and balance the meal with potassium-rich vegetables to mitigate sodium-related vascular effects.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Zhajiangmian (炸酱面, literally 'fried sauce noodles') is one of the most iconic dishes of Beijing cuisine, with a culinary history spanning several centuries. The dish consists of thick, hand-pulled or machine-cut wheat noodles served topped with a rich, savoury sauce made by stir-frying ground or diced pork with fermented yellow soybean paste (huangdoujiang) and sweet bean sauce (tianmianjiang) in oil, often with aromatics such as garlic, ginger, and scallions. The sauce is dark brown, deeply umami, slightly sweet, and intensely flavoured. The noodles are typically boiled until tender and served warm, with the sauce ladled on top and mixed at the table. Traditional accompaniments include julienned cucumber, bean sprouts, radish, and edamame, which provide textural contrast and freshness. Nutritionally, zhajiangmian is a moderate-energy dish providing approximately 155 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution from pork (approximately 7.2 g/100 g) and a moderate carbohydrate load from wheat noodles (approximately 19.5 g/100 g). The dish is high in sodium due to the fermented paste base, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 47 (low), consistent with wheat noodle-based dishes, though the glycemic load per standard serving is moderate at approximately 36. Zhajiangmian is not suitable for individuals with gluten or soy allergies, and is not halal due to pork content. It is a culturally significant comfort food widely served in Beijing-style restaurants across China and in Chinese diaspora communities globally, including Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or Chinese Food Composition Table accessible in full) provides a direct composite entry for zhajiangmian as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked wheat noodles and ground pork with soybean paste, cross-referenced against published Chinese Food Composition Table ranges for this dish. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (wheat noodles, Chinese-style) in the University of Sydney GI Database per Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish in the component sources used; pork and noodles contribute negligible vitamin D and the value is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for estimation. 2. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is set to 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in pork and vegetable oil preparations of this type per USDA data. 3. Sodium content is notably high due to fermented soybean paste and soy sauce; restaurant preparations may vary significantly. 4. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal. 5. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate from wheat noodles (Chinese-style, boiled) per Atkinson et al. 2008; no direct GI measurement for zhajiangmian as a composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate. 7. This dish is served in Singapore by at least one restaurant (See Ya Tonight 今晚有局) and is classified as Chinese cuisine; multilingual names are provided in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean multilingual naming rules. 8. The food_id GD000819 is preserved from the input seed record.
